summary: Ukrainian FPV drones struck the Starobilsk college in the LPR, killing at least six civilians and prompting a Russian call for a military response. Russian forces launched a massive Shahed‑type drone swarm, with Ukrainian air defenses claiming 102 kills across the north, south and east. The United States approved a $108 million sale of HAWK air‑defence equipment to Ukraine, while Russian attacks continued in Marhanets and the Grushovaya oil terminal in Novorossiysk.
key_points: Ukrainian FPV drones attacked the Starobilsk professional college in LPR, collapsing the building and causing 4‑6 civilian deaths and 39 injuries (Source: @tass_agency/377177).
Ukrainian forces reported shooting down 102 hostile Shahed, Gerbera, Italmas and imitation drones during a large‑scale swarm attack (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
The United States approved a $108 million sale of HAWK anti‑aircraft missile system equipment to Ukraine (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Russian air strike on Marhanets injured 13 people, damaging a civilian vehicle with an FPV drone (Source: @UkraineNow/81386).
Three FPV drones were observed over the Black Sea near the Lazurnoye area, indicating continued maritime UAV activity (Source: @eRadarrua/93102).
Russian forces attacked the Grushovaya oil terminal in Novorossiysk, igniting a fire at a facility storing ~1.2 million tonnes of fuel (Source: @UkraineNow/81392).
Ukrainian General Staff stated the Starobilsk strike targeted a Russian drone‑operating headquarters, not civilian infrastructure (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).
Putin labeled the Starobilsk college attack a terrorist act and ordered the Russian Ministry of Defence to prepare a response (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30369).

sections.executive_summary: Ukrainian FPV drones struck the Starobilsk college in the LPR, killing at least six civilians and prompting a Russian call for a military response. Ukrainian air defenses claimed to have downed 102 hostile drones during a massive Shahed‑type swarm across multiple fronts. The United States approved a $108 million HAWK air‑defence package for Ukraine, while Russian strikes continued in Marhanets and at the Grushovaya oil terminal in Novorossiysk.

points: Ukrainian FPV drones targeted the Starobilsk professional college, collapsing the building and causing 4‑6 civilian deaths and 39 injuries, according to local officials (Source: @tass_agency/377177).
The Ukrainian General Staff asserted the strike was aimed at a Russian unit headquarters operating the drones, not at civilian facilities (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).
Ukrainian forces reported 13 injured in Marhanets after a Russian FPV drone struck a vehicle carrying plant workers, causing medium‑severity wounds (Source: @UkraineNow/81386).
Three FPV drones were tracked over the Black Sea near Lazurnoye, suggesting continued maritime UAV operations against Ukrainian assets (Source: @eRadarrua/93102).
Ukrainian air‑defence units, supported by aviation and electronic‑warfare units, intercepted 102 hostile drones during a coordinated swarm attack (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
Ukrainian authorities are preparing to integrate the newly approved HAWK missile system, enhancing medium‑range air defence capabilities (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Ukrainian radar and acoustic networks reported multiple Shahed‑type drone incursions north of Sumy, Chernihiv and Kyiv, prompting rapid engagement (multiple posts).
Russian leadership, via President Putin, condemned the Starobilsk college attack as a terrorist act and ordered the Ministry of Defence to prepare a military response (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30369).
Russian forces launched a large‑scale drone swarm comprising Shahed, Gerbera, Italmas and imitation types from directions including Kursk, Shatalovo, Bryansk and Crimea (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
A Russian air strike on Marhanets resulted in 13 civilian injuries, demonstrating continued use of FPV drones against infrastructure (Source: @UkraineNow/81386).
Russian naval aviation targeted the Grushovaya oil terminal in Novorossiysk, igniting a fire at a facility with ~1.2 million tonnes of fuel storage (Source: @UkraineNow/81392).
Russian electronic‑warfare units reported successful suppression of Ukrainian drone activity in the north, south and east during the May 22‑23 swarm (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
Russian propaganda outlets cited the Starobilsk incident as a provocation, attempting to frame Ukrainian actions as attacks on civilians (Source: @tass_agency/377177).
Russian forces continued to deploy Shahed‑type drones from Crimea towards Odesa and the southern front, threatening maritime logistics (multiple posts).
Ukrainian FPV drones struck the Starobilsk college in the LPR, a key eastern hotspot, causing multiple civilian casualties (Source: @tass_agency/377177).
Russian Shahed swarm launched from Crimea and Kursk penetrated the eastern sector, with Ukrainian air defence reporting 102 kills across the front (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
Marhanets, located in the Dnipropetrovsk region, suffered a drone strike that injured 13 civilians, indicating eastern industrial targets remain vulnerable (Source: @UkraineNow/81386).
Ukrainian radar detected Shahed drones heading toward the industrial zones of Hryshynivka and Sofiyivka in Dnipropetrovsk, prompting intercepts (Source: @eRadarrua/93114).
Ukrainian forces reported drone activity over the Sumy‑Khrustalnyi corridor, with several Shahed drones intercepted near the border (Source: @eRadarrua/93110).
Russian electronic‑warfare units claimed successful jamming of Ukrainian UAVs over the Luhansk‑Donetsk border area (multiple posts).
Ukrainian artillery units engaged incoming drones near the town of Hryshynivka, reporting no damage to civilian infrastructure (multiple posts).
Ukrainian acoustic sensors reported hostile drones over northern Sumy and Chernihiv regions, with several Shahed‑type UAVs intercepted (Source: @kpszsu/62455).
Drone sightings from Zhytomyr heading toward Kyiv region were logged, prompting air‑defence alerts in the north (Source: @kpszsu/62469).
Ukrainian forces shot down multiple Shahed drones over the north‑east of Poltava, heading westward (Source: @kpszsu/62458).
A Shahed swarm approached the Kyiv oblast from the north, with one UAV tracked over Borova before being neutralised (Source: @eRadarrua/93107).
Ukrainian radar detected a Shahed drone crossing from Belarus into the Chernihiv region, intercepted near Kulykivka (Source: @eRadarrua/93088).
Ukrainian air‑defence units reported 12 successful strikes on Shahed drones across nine northern locations during the May 22‑23 attack (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
Ukrainian forces deployed mobile fire groups to protect critical infrastructure in the north, successfully engaging incoming UAVs (multiple posts).
Three FPV drones were observed over the Black Sea near Lazurnoye, indicating continued UAV activity threatening southern maritime routes (Source: @eRadarrua/93102).
Russian Shahed drones launched from Crimea moved southward toward Odesa, with some heading for the town of Tarasbunar, prompting interceptions (Source: @kpszsu/62477).
Ukrainian air‑defence reported downing of Shahed drones over the southern front, including near the oil terminal Grushovaya in Novorossiysk, though the terminal was hit (Source: @UkraineNow/81392).
Ukrainian forces engaged a Shahed swarm over the Kherson region, preventing damage to the Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant's backup line (multiple posts).
Ukrainian radar logged a Shahed UAV crossing from the Black Sea toward the Mykolaiv area, intercepted before reaching critical ports (multiple posts).
Ukrainian mobile anti‑air units were positioned near the southern rail hub in Mykolaiv to counter UAV threats (multiple posts).
Russian electronic‑warfare units attempted to jam Ukrainian UAV detection systems along the southern coastline, with limited success (multiple posts).

Ukrainian air‑defence forces claimed to have shot down 102 hostile Shahed, Gerbera, Italmas and imitation drones during a coordinated swarm attack across the north, south and east (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
The United States approved a $108 million sale of HAWK anti‑aircraft missile system equipment to Ukraine, enhancing medium‑range air‑defence capability (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Ukrainian aviation units provided air cover during the drone swarm, engaging high‑altitude Shahed UAVs over the Kyiv and Sumy regions (multiple posts).
Russian forces employed a mix of Shahed‑type kamikaze drones and electronic‑warfare jamming to degrade Ukrainian radar and SAM performance (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
Ukrainian electronic‑warfare units successfully suppressed several Russian drone control links, reducing the number of inbound UAVs (multiple posts).
Ukrainian mobile fire groups equipped with MANPADS engaged low‑altitude Shahed drones over the southern front, reporting multiple kills (multiple posts).
Ukrainian air‑defence reported 12 confirmed strikes on Shahed drones across nine locations during the May 22‑23 attack, with debris falling in five areas (Source: @kpszsu/62474).

key_developments: Ukrainian FPV drones struck the Starobilsk college in the LPR, collapsing the building and killing six civilians while injuring dozens (Source: @tass_agency/377177).
Ukrainian air‑defence forces reported shooting down 102 hostile Shahed‑type drones during a large‑scale swarm attack across multiple fronts (Source: @kpszsu/62474).
The United States approved a $108 million sale of HAWK air‑defence missile system equipment to Ukraine, bolstering its medium‑range air‑defence (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30358).
Russian forces attacked the Grushovaya oil terminal in Novorossiysk, igniting a fire at a facility storing approximately 1.2 million tonnes of fuel (Source: @UkraineNow/81392).
A Russian air strike on Marhanets injured 13 civilians, with an FPV drone striking a vehicle carrying plant workers (Source: @UkraineNow/81386).
President Vladimir Putin labeled the Starobilsk college attack a terrorist act and ordered the Russian Ministry of Defence to prepare a military response (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30369).
Ukrainian General Staff asserted the Starobilsk strike targeted a Russian drone‑operating headquarters, not civilian infrastructure (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30372).
sections.threat_assessment: Ukrainian UAV operations continue to target Russian‑held infrastructure in the LPR, while Russian Shahed swarms test Ukraine's layered air‑defence. The influx of US‑supplied HAWK systems will improve medium‑range interception but may not fully counter the high volume of low‑cost kamikaze drones. Russian strikes on energy assets in Novorossiysk demonstrate an expanded reach, threatening logistics beyond the Ukrainian theatre.
sections.regional_response: Western allies are accelerating arms deliveries, highlighted by the HAWK package, while diplomatic pressure mounts on Russia over civilian casualties in Starobilsk. Russia is leveraging propaganda to frame Ukrainian attacks as terrorist acts, seeking to justify escalated military measures. Neighboring states remain vigilant, with increased radar and acoustic monitoring along their borders to detect cross‑border UAV incursions.
sections.intelligence_notes: Open‑source monitoring shows a pattern of Russian drone launches from Crimea and western Russian regions targeting both Ukrainian and Russian infrastructure, indicating a dual‑theater strategy. Ukrainian acoustic and radar networks are increasingly effective, as evidenced by the high number of intercepted drones, but gaps remain in southern maritime coverage. Continued funding and integration of Western air‑defence assets will be critical to sustain Ukraine's defensive posture against evolving UAV threats.
